title: The Diggerן¿½s Song
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scrape the bottom of the hole: gather up the stuff,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fossick in the crannies, lest you leave a grain behind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just another shovelful and thatן¿½ll be enough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now weן¿½ll take it to the bank and see what we can find,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give the dish a twirl around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let the water swirl around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gently let it circulate, thereן¿½s music in the swish,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the tinkle of the gravel,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the pebbles quickly travel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Around in merry circles on the bottom of the dish.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah, if man could only wash his life, if he only could,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Panning off the evil deeds, keeping but the good,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What a mighty lot of diggerן¿½s dishes would be sold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though I fear the heap of tailings would be greater than the gold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give the dish a twirl around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let the water swirl around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Manן¿½s the sport of circumstance however he may wish,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fortune! are you there now?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Answer to my prayer now,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And drop a half ounce nugget in the bottom of the dish.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gently let the water lap, keep the corners dry,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s about the place the gold will generally stay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What was that bright particle that just then caught my eye?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I fear me by the look of things ן¿½Twas only yellow clay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just another twirl around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let the water swirl around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s the way we rob the river of its golden fish,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whatן¿½s that? canן¿½t we snare a one?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Donן¿½t say that thereן¿½s neן¿½er a one,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bah, thereן¿½s not a colour in the bottom of the dish.
meaning:
About: Barcroft Henry Thomas Boake (March 26, 1866 ן¿½ found dead May 10, 1892) was an Australian poet.

Born in Sydney, Boake worked as a surveyor and a boundary rider in New South Wales and Queensland, but is best remembered for his poetry, a volume of which was published five years after his death. A sufferer of bipolar disorder, he is believed to have committed suicide. His body was found hanging by the neck eight days after he disappeared on May 2, 1892, from a stockwhip at Middle Harbour in Sydney. One writer on Boake's life has mentioned that the suicide took place during the 1891-93 depression when the poet was unable to find work, also noting that "it has been suggested that he killed himself for the love of one of the McKeahnie girls," sisters of the horseman Charlie McKeahnie.
